skill.name: gpt-image-2
skill.description: Generate and edit images with OpenAI GPT Image 2 through the RunComfy CLI. The skill documents prompt patterns, supported sizes, edit workflows, and service limitations.
skill.summary: Create GPT Image 2 generations and edits through RunComfy.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but most are Markdown code spans, fenced command examples, URL examples, or ellipsis in documented API paths. The confirmed residual risk is legitimate but material: the skill instructs agents to run a local CLI, use a RunComfy bearer token, store that token locally, and send prompts or image URLs to RunComfy endpoints.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party CLI Execution and Data Transfer
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to install and run the RunComfy CLI, then submit prompts and image URLs to RunComfy model endpoints with a bearer token. This matches the skill purpose, but it needs user awareness because local command execution and third-party content transfer are required.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and RunComfy API workflow are explicitly documented. The behavior is legitimate for the image-generation skill, but the security impact is real because user content and credentials are involved.

medium_findings.title: Local Bearer Token Storage
medium_findings.description: The skill documents that RunComfy login writes an API token under the user's home configuration directory. The stated file mode is owner-only, but marketplace users should still treat this as credential storage.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token storage path and permissions are directly described in the skill. There is no evidence of theft, but persistent credential storage is a meaningful operational risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Formatting False Positives
low_findings.description: Most static external-command detections are Markdown inline code, table values, sample prompts, or fenced examples. No hidden script file or automatic execution path was found in the reviewed skill content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious backticks are visible Markdown formatting and command examples. The repository shard contains only SKILL.md, so no executable implementation was present.

low_findings.title: Expected Hardcoded Service URLs
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings point to RunComfy homepage, documentation, model endpoints, or placeholder public image URLs. These URLs support the documented service workflow and do not show covert exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs align with the stated RunComfy integration. They are not disguised domains, callbacks, or unrelated collection endpoints.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detections Are URL Ellipsis
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are caused by documented placeholder API paths that use ellipsis before request routes. They are not filesystem traversal operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text appears in prose explaining HTTP polling and result endpoints. No file read, file write, or path concatenation logic is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Unconfirmed
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography hits do not correspond to cryptographic primitives in the reviewed lines. The referenced content is front matter and exit-code documentation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No MD5, SHA1, cipher selection, hashing API, or password handling code appears at the cited locations. These look like scanner keyword collisions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Manual External CLI Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs agents to install and run RunComfy CLI commands. Arguments are shown as JSON input, but execution should remain visible to the user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CLI commands are explicit and central to the skill. The examples do not show shell concatenation with untrusted input, which lowers injection concern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Outbound Model API Requests
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented workflow sends requests to RunComfy model API endpoints and downloads generated outputs from allowed RunComfy hosts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The outbound endpoints are clearly named in the skill. They are expected for this integration, but they still expose prompts and referenced media to a third-party service.

dangerous_patterns.title: Persistent Local Token File
dangerous_patterns.description: The RunComfy login flow stores the service token in a hidden configuration file under the user's home directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The token file location is directly documented. The stated owner-only permissions reduce, but do not remove, credential exposure risk.

content.user_title: Generate GPT Image 2 Assets on RunComfy
content.value_statement: Teams need predictable image prompts that preserve text, layout, and brand details. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through GPT Image 2 generation and editing on RunComfy.
actual_capabilities: Routes GPT Image 2 generation requests to the RunComfy text-to-image endpoint.
Routes image editing requests to the RunComfy edit endpoint with public HTTPS references.
Documents the three supported output sizes for generation.
Explains when to use automatic aspect-ratio preservation for edits.
Provides prompt patterns for embedded text, signage, packaging, and layout control.
Compares GPT Image 2 with sibling image models for model selection.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a working RunComfy account.
Sends prompts and image URLs to RunComfy rather than directly to OpenAI.
Text-to-image supports only three fixed sizes.
Multi-image editing is guidance based and is not a precise control stack.

use_cases.title: Create Product Visuals
use_cases.description: Generate product stills with controlled labels, lighting, and clean commercial framing.

use_cases.title: Localize Brand Assets
use_cases.description: Edit existing concepts into variants with short multilingual text and preserved composition.

use_cases.title: Prototype Visual Layouts
use_cases.description: Create UI mockups, labeled diagrams, or poster concepts where text placement matters.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Product Image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a clean product photo of a matte water bottle on a neutral studio background. Add the label text "AQUA+" clearly.

prompt_templates.title: Edited Background
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the provided product image. Keep the product shape, logo, and label unchanged. Replace the background with a bright studio sweep.

prompt_templates.title: Localized Signage
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a cafe storefront at dusk. The main sign reads "COFFEE" and the window decal reads "コーヒー" in Japanese kana.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Reference Composition
prompt_templates.prompt: Use image 1 as the subject and image 2 as the room. Match the lighting from image 2 while preserving the subject identity.

output_examples.input: Create a product hero image for a ceramic bottle with the text AQUA+.
output_examples.output: A generated product still with a centered bottle, readable AQUA+ label, soft window light, and neutral commercial styling.

output_examples.input: Edit a product photo to use a white studio background while keeping the package unchanged.
output_examples.output: An edited image that preserves the package shape, logo, and typography while replacing the background with a clean studio sweep.

output_examples.input: Create a small cafe storefront with Japanese signage.
output_examples.output: A storefront concept with warm interior light and a visible Japanese kana sign placed on the front facade.

best_practices: Quote embedded text exactly and keep it short.
Preserve identity, logos, and composition explicitly in edit prompts.
Change one attribute per iteration when refining an image.
anti_patterns: Do not combine conflicting text instructions in one prompt.
Do not submit private images or confidential prompts without approval.
Do not expect unsupported aspect ratios from text-to-image.

faq.question: Does this skill call OpenAI directly?
faq.answer: No. It uses RunComfy endpoints that host GPT Image 2 workflows.

faq.question: Does it require a RunComfy account?
faq.answer: Yes. The RunComfy CLI must be installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Can it edit existing images?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports the documented edit endpoint with public HTTPS image references.

faq.question: What image sizes are supported?
faq.answer: Text-to-image supports 1024 by 1024, 1024 by 1536, and 1536 by 1024.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential assets?
faq.answer: Use caution. Prompts and referenced images are sent to a third-party model service.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it requires local CLI execution, network access, and token handling.
